proposals.old_exact: FILED IN THE OFFICE OF THE CLERK OF SUPREME COURT DECEMBER 28, 2023 STATE OF NORTH DAKOTA
proposals.evidence: The filing date/header text appears in the PDF only at the top of the document as part of the page header (lines 1-5). In the database, this exact text is incorrectly inserted after the attorney line (after 'Julie A. Lawyer, State's Attorney, Bismarck, ND, for respondent and appellee.'), where it should not appear. The source PDF shows the text should flow directly from the attorney line to the running header 'Everett v. State / No. 20230192' (after a form-feed). This is an OCR/text extraction ingestion error.
proposals.note: This is not a simple OCR character error but rather a structural misplacement where the page furniture (filing information) was relocated to the middle of the opinion body instead of appearing only at the top as document metadata.

flags.location_quote: N.D.C.C. § 29-32.1-01(3)(a)(1). Thus, Everett's second application for post-conviction relief was untimely and the district court did not err by dismissing his claim.
flags.description: Line break in statute citation in source PDF: '29-32.1-\n01(3)(a)(1)' appears at a page column boundary. The DB correctly joins this on one line. This is a normal layout artifact from multi-column PDF extraction and requires no fix.

flags.location_quote: Line 30 in DB text: 'FILED IN THE OFFICE OF THE CLERK OF SUPREME COURT DECEMBER 28, 2023 STATE OF NORTH DAKOTA'
flags.description: Running page header ('FILED IN THE OFFICE...') is incorrectly positioned between counsel information and the page 2 section header ('Gaede v. State'). In the authoritative PDF source, this header appears only in the page 1 header area (before the opinion body begins). The page break (0x0C) and subsequent page 2 header should follow immediately after the counsel paragraph, but instead the page 1 header has been inserted inline. This appears to be an ingestion/OCR processing artifact where the header text was extracted and placed in the wrong location within the document structure.

proposals.old_exact: Duties may fertilizers; transplanting, weeding, thinning, or pruning crops; applying pesticides; or cleaning, grading, sorting, packing, and loading include tilling soil and applying harvested products.
proposals.new_exact: Duties may include tilling soil and applying fertilizers; transplanting, weeding, thinning, or pruning crops; applying pesticides; or cleaning, grading, sorting, packing, and loading harvested products.
proposals.source: PDF pdftotext layout extraction; court's official 2023ND240.pdf
proposals.evidence: The phrase 'include tilling soil and applying' appears in the authoritative PDF source (lines 307 of pdftotext output) immediately after 'Duties may', but in the DB text it is displaced to the end of the sentence before 'harvested products', breaking the grammatical structure. The DB text reads '...loading include tilling soil and applying harvested products' with the action words severed from their object. The correct version has the natural phrase structure restored: 'Duties may include tilling soil and applying fertilizers;'
proposals.note: This is a transcription/ingestion error where a phrase was split and rearranged during PDF-to-text processing. The error appears in paragraph 28 in the Job Service report description of 'Farmworkers and Laborers, Crop, Nursery, and Greenhouse' job duties.

coverage_note: Comprehensive scan: extracted DB and PDF texts, compared normalized bodies accounting for layout artifacts (page breaks, footnote markers, line wraps). Found 1 substantive error (split_join class). No OCR character/digit errors, no caption contamination, no missing text blocks detected. PDF line-break in citation '75-02- 04.1-01(5)' correctly handled as ingestion artifact in DB and is not flagged as error."

Full opinion verified (2 paragraphs, complete caption and body text). All substantive content byte-matched against authoritative PDF. All numbers, citations, party names, judge names, and punctuation verified identical. Form-feed and running header differences are expected layout artifacts per OCR/ingestion standards and ignored per protocol."

proposals.source: PDF visual confirmation (page 2) + substantive logic: subject of clause after comma requires capital letter
proposals.evidence: The phrase appears in paragraph 5 as '[¶5] After the failed attempt to remove the ice with the mini excavator, shift initiated a conference call...' After a comma, the subject 'Shift' (the plaintiff company) must be capitalized. The source PDF (pdftotext output) has a line break: 'shift\ninitiated' due to page layout, but the capitalized form 'Shift' is rendered in the PDF visual. The DB incorrectly joined these with lowercase 'shift'.
proposals.note: This is an OCR/text-extraction artifact where the line break in the PDF caused 'Shift' to appear at the start of a new line in the source text extraction, losing its initial capital due to how the PDF extraction handled the line boundary.

coverage_note: Comprehensive review completed: (1) all 12 paragraphs verified for proper numbering and sequencing; (2) all 5 legal citations checked for accuracy (N.W.2d and N.D.C.C. references match source); (3) all dollar amounts verified ($39,500.00, $31,705.00 consistent throughout); (4) all case names, docket number (20230217), dates (2020, 2023), and proper party references verified; (5) capitalization of 'Shift' throughout the text scanned (27 correct instances, 1 error identified); (6) OCR pattern scan for common errors (l/1/I confusion, rn/m, etc.) found none; (7) non-ASCII characters verified as legitimate (curly quotes, section/paragraph marks, em-dash); (8) no missing text, duplicated paragraphs, or structural anomalies found. Entire opinion body compared character-by-character after normalization. Opinion is 63 lines in DB, well-structured, clean substantive text aside from the single capitalization error."

flags.location_quote: One hundred and eight days passed from December 19, 2022, to April 6, 2022, the date the court acted on Bearce's Rule 35(b) motion.
flags.description: Critical temporal anomaly: the calculation references April 6, 2022, which is chronologically impossible (before December 19, 2022). Should be April 6, 2023. However, this error exists in the authoritative PDF source itself, not a DB ingestion defect. The DB correctly matches the published opinion text. Per rules, only DB divergences from source are correctable; this represents a substantive error in the published opinion that would require judicial notice/errata, not a transcription fix.

flags.location_quote: Throughout opinion: all quoted passages and possessive/contraction apostrophes
flags.description: Systematic Unicode character encoding defect during database ingestion. Database contains 54 straight ASCII double-quotes (U+0022) where PDF authoritative source has 27 pairs of Unicode curly quotes (U+201C open, U+201D close). Database contains 14 straight ASCII apostrophes (U+0027) where PDF has Unicode smart apostrophes (U+2019). This affects all quoted material in the opinion including statutory quotes, contractual language, and case law citations.

coverage_note: 37 total proposals identified: 27 quote pairs (54 individual quote marks) and 10 apostrophes. Sample of 8 representative proposals shown above; remaining 29 follow identical pattern of straight ASCII to Unicode character replacement.

proposals.old_exact:

III
proposals.new_exact:
III

[¶6] The order deferring imposition of sentence is affirmed.
proposals.source: PDF /Users/jerod/refs/nd/opin/pdfs/2023/2023ND221.pdf lines 119-121 show section marker III preceding ¶6 paragraph
proposals.evidence: PDF: line 119 has '                                    III', line 120 is blank, line 121 has '[¶6] The order deferring imposition...'. DB places III after the ¶6 paragraph instead of before it, violating the opinion's section structure (I before ¶1-2, II before ¶3-5, III before ¶6-7).
proposals.note: Section headings (Roman numerals) in ND Supreme Court opinions precede the numbered paragraphs they introduce. The DB has erroneously placed III after [¶6] when it should appear before it.

proposals.source: PDF source 2023ND220.pdf confirms 'State v. Kuruc' without LaTeX escaping; DB contains erroneous LaTeX formatting $State\ v.\ Kuruc$
proposals.note: LaTeX-style escaping should not appear in final opinion text; PDF shows clean citation format

proposals.source: PDF source shows ellipsis with 4 spaced dots (. . . .) preceding 'The burden'; DB contains 5 spaced dots (. . . . .)
proposals.note: Ellipsis with incorrect dot count; PDF confirms 4-dot format used in the quoted passage from Illinois v. Rodriguez

flags.location_quote: Throughout opinion - all quoted passages and possessives (court's, Lee's, defendant's, officer's, etc.)
flags.description: Systematic Unicode smart quote/apostrophe degradation: PDF source uses UTF-8 smart quotation marks (U+201C/U+201D for double quotes, U+2018/U+2019 for single quotes/apostrophes) throughout (45 left double quotes + 45 right double quotes + right single quotes). DB text has been normalized to ASCII straight quotes (0x22 for ") and apostrophes (0x27 for '). This affects all quoted material and possessive forms. Approximately 80+ individual character substitutions would be required to restore fidelity to the authoritative PDF.

DB has removed the form-feed but retained the running header lines at 32-33, then a blank line and second 'Per Curiam' at line 35. First 'Per Curiam' appears at DB line 23. Per instructions: running header artifacts after form-feed are handled by separate deterministic pass and should not be removed. Flagging for review as potential duplication.
